The inspiration for this tool came from an excellent community contribution by fellow SAP experts. If you haven't read it yet, I highly recommend checking out the blog post BTP FinOps: Keeping track of your credits by  @Willem_Pardaens .

That post showcases a brilliant application for overall SAP BTP FinOps, helping organizations track their global credit consumption. It got me thinking: as Generative AI explodes, we need an equally robust, highly specialized "double-click" into the specific, token-heavy workloads running inside SAP BTP AI Core. The AI Core Consumption Monitor was built to fill that exact operational niche.

The Challenge: Visibility in a Generative AI World

When utilizing Large Language Models (LLMs) and other foundation models via SAP BTP AI Core, costs are primarily driven by token consumption. For enterprise architecture and finance teams, tracking raw API usage isn't enough. They need a clear mapping of how input text, output generations, and advanced features—such as grounding data for RAG pipelines—translate into billing units.

Without granular tracking, organizations risk unexpected cost spikes from data-heavy prompts, making it difficult to allocate budgets accurately across multiple lines of business or calculate the true ROI of internal AI implementations.

Introducing the AI Core Consumption Monitor

The SAP BTP AI Core Consumption Monitor is an analytical dashboard crafted to give you 360-degree visibility into your AI ecosystem. It acts as a centralized "single pane of glass" for administrators to observe, analyze, and optimize their AI consumption.

Here are the core capabilities available in the application today:

1. Granular Token Tracking

The application breaks down your consumption into granular metrics. Instead of viewing a single consolidated figure, you get a clear bifurcation of input and output tokens for each Model . This detail is crucial because foundation models often charge different rates for the prompt (input) versus the generation (output).

2. Capacity Unit (CU) Correlation

SAP BTP services consume credits via Capacity Units (CUs). The dashboard maps your token usage directly to the corresponding Capacity Unit consumption. This bridges the gap between technical metrics (tokens) and financial impact (CUs), giving procurement and IT leaders exact data for budgeting and resource optimization.

3. Proactive Notifications

To ensure you never get an unexpected bill at the end of the month, the application allows you to set up notifications. You can establish thresholds based on your expected consumption patterns and receive alerts before usage gets out of hand.

Technical Architecture

The application is built keeping SAP best practices in mind, ensuring it securely communicates with your SAP BTP environment while providing a responsive, analytical user experience.

 

Here is a quick look at the tech stack powering this monitor:

  • Frontend: [Vite React] for a clean, filterable dashboard interface.

  • Backend: Built using the [ SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) / Node.js] to handle data aggregation, orchestrate API requests, and execute token-to-CU calculations.

  • Database: [SAP HANA Cloud ] to securely store historical consumption data for long-term trend analysis.

  • Integration: Utilizes standard Usage Data Management Service (UAS ) API to securely fetch usage details.

What’s Next? The Roadmap

This is just the beginning. As SAP BTP AI Core and enterprise FinOps requirements evolve, this consumption monitor will evolve alongside them. I am actively working on expanding its capabilities to make it even more robust for complex enterprise environments.

Future improvements include:

CMS IntegrationIntegration with Cloud Management Service (CMS) for fetching sub account information through API — enabling automatic discovery and onboarding of subaccounts
Robust Notification SystemsUpgrading the alert mechanism to support complex threshold rules and direct integration with enterprise communication and automation channels.

Get Started Today!

The project is fully open-source and hosted as an official SAP Sample. I invite you to deploy it in your landscape, test it out, and share your feedback.

  • Explore the Code: GitHub Repository (SAP-samples/btp-ai-core-consumption-monitor)

  • Contribute: If you have ideas for features, want to help with notification integrations, or find any bugs , feel free to open an issue or submit a pull request!

    •  Bugs — Please create an issue in this repository
    •  Feature Requests — Open an issue with the enhancement label

How is your organization currently managing and tracking its AI Core consumption? Have you started implementing FinOps frameworks for your cloud credit management?

entry.title: Building Multi-Agent Systems for Production: The State, Context, and Memory Playbook
entry.content:
Blog 6 of N: Building Multi-Agent Systems for Production - The State, Context, and Memory Playbook

Every article on multi-agent orchestration uses the words state, context, and memory as if they were the same thing. They are not. Mixing them up is why your agent forgets what the user said two turns ago, why your storage bill quietly doubles every month, and why your "memory feature" leaks data across users.

This blog is framework-free. Whatever library you use, these patterns do not change. The next blog will cover the five pillars of a production agent - evaluation, observability, reliability, security, cost. State, context, and memory sit underneath all five, so we start here.

The vocabulary in one minute

  • Context = tokens the model sees this one call. Lives for milliseconds. Analogy: what is on your screen right now.
  • State = data your orchestration carries between steps of the current task. Lives for seconds to minutes. Analogy: variables in a running program.
  • Memory = knowledge that persists across tasks and sessions. Lives for days to forever. Analogy: what is on your disk.

"Trim the messages" is a context operation. "Delete checkpoints after 30 days" is a state operation. "Purge user data on a GDPR request" is a memory operation. Different tools, different retention, different failure modes.

The four kinds of memory

  • Working - what the agent is holding for the current task. Lives in the context window.
  • Episodic - time-stamped log of what happened. Past conversations, past incidents.
  • Semantic - stable facts, stripped of the episode they were learned in. "User's timezone is IST." "Company policy is 30-day refunds."
  • Procedural - how to do things. Playbooks, workflows, tool-use strategies. The least mature in production today.

Each kind needs its own store and its own retention. Mixing logs and facts in one vector index poisons retrieval - a query for "timezone" starts pulling back conversations about timezones.

Context engineering, in three lines

Anthropic's definition, worth quoting verbatim: "Context engineering refers to the set of strategies for curating and maintaining the optimal set of tokens (information) during LLM inference."

Prompt engineering is phrasing one instruction. Context engineering is deciding what the model sees at all during one call - which past messages, which retrieved documents, which tool outputs, in which order. Prompt engineering is a piece inside context engineering, not a peer to it.

Rule of thumb: five to ten items per turn retrieved from long-term stores. More crowds out reasoning; less causes amnesia.

Two stores, not one

Every mature agent product uses two:

  • Store A - source of truth. Your app database: conversations, messages, artifacts. What the UI reads. Retention driven by product policy (indefinite until user deletes).
  • Store B - execution cache. Your framework's checkpoint store: state snapshots so a crashed task can resume. Retention driven by ops (aggressive TTL).

The trap: pointing the UI at Store B and skipping Store A. It works for thirty days. Then your nightly cleanup prunes old checkpoints and users open conversations that are now empty. Chat history was living inside the crash-resume cache.

Rule: if deleting your execution cache would delete user-visible history, you have one store where you needed two.

The production patterns

Everything above is setup. This section is the point. Each pattern is a problem you will hit in production and the pattern that solves it.

To keep the examples concrete, we will use one running scenario throughout: a Coordinator agent takes user questions from a chat UI and dispatches work to two peer agents - Sherlock (qualitative signals: logs, traces, deploys, runbooks) and Watson (quantitative signals: metrics, baselines, postmortems). Coordinator plans, Sherlock and Watson investigate, Coordinator synthesises the answer. Every problem and pattern below is framed against this trio.

The handoff contract

When the Coordinator dispatches work to Sherlock or Watson, do not pass a paragraph of prose. Pass a typed, versioned envelope that both sides agree on. At minimum it carries:

  • The goal in one sentence ("investigate the checkout 5xx spike"), not a step-by-step script.
  • The scope: what is in bounds, what is out.
  • The definition of done: when the peer knows it is finished.
  • A compressed context snippet from the conversation - not the full transcript.
  • The user preferences that the peer needs (timezone, focus service, format).
  • A deadline: how much time the peer has to answer.
  • A schema version: so the peer can reject envelopes it does not understand instead of guessing.

When the peer responds, its envelope carries not just the result but a status: success, partial success, or failure. On partial success it lists what was completed, what was not, and what side effects were already committed - so the Coordinator knows whether to retry, compensate, or escalate. A boolean success/fail flag is a bug generator. A typed status with partial output is a debuggable system.

Concrete example. Coordinator asks Sherlock: "Investigate checkout 5xx at 14:32. In scope: logs and traces. Out of scope: metrics - Watson is handling those. Deadline: 3 seconds. User cares about checkout-svc only." Sherlock returns: "Partial success. Ran log search, found the signature. Runbook lookup timed out. Side effects: none. Recommend retrying runbook lookup on the next turn." Coordinator now has enough to answer and enough to plan the next turn. Nothing was lost.

One thread per conversation, one thread per task

Every agent needs a thread identifier so its state is isolated from other conversations. The trap is using the same identifier at every layer. The Coordinator's conversation with the user lives for weeks; Sherlock's involvement in one turn lives for three seconds. Give them different lifetimes.

A pattern that works: the Coordinator's thread is the conversation identifier itself. Sherlock and Watson, for each task, get a thread identifier like conversation:sherlock:task-42 or conversation:watson:task-42. The prefix lets you grep for everything Sherlock did inside one conversation. The suffix keeps peer state per task, not per conversation. Peer threads can be aggressively pruned; the Coordinator's conversation thread is durable.

Context offloading with artifact refs (never store the same blob twice)

This pattern has a name in the literature: context offloading with artifact references. The idea is to keep the model's context small by pushing big payloads out to a side store and passing only pointers.

Sherlock's search-logs tool returns two hundred log lines. Without discipline that payload ends up in Sherlock's checkpoint, in the response envelope back to Coordinator, in the Coordinator's checkpoint, in the app's messages table, and in the streamed UI response. Five copies of the same blob. Storage doubles quietly for six months, then someone notices.

The fix: Sherlock's tool returns a small content summary for the model plus a separate artifact reference for the store. Big payloads are written once, in one authoritative place (typically the Coordinator's artifacts table, since it owns the conversation). Every other layer - envelopes, checkpoints, UI stream - carries only the artifact ID plus a short summary. "200 log entries, dominant signature 'redis timeout,' full payload at artifact-a3b1." The model reads the summary; the pointer is available if it needs to drill in; the storage bill stays flat. One authoritative copy, many cheap references.

One deadline, propagated everywhere

Your service has an eight-second SLA to the user. Sherlock and Watson do not know that. Unless the deadline is passed explicitly, each peer will happily spend its full internal budget on tool calls and blow the SLA.

The pattern: the Coordinator creates a single deadline object at the moment the request enters the system. It is attached to every downstream call as a header. Every stage - Coordinator's classifier, Sherlock, Watson, Coordinator's aggregator - checks the deadline before every model call and before every tool call. If less than half a second remains, refuse to start a new tool call and return what you have. Budget it: intake takes a small slice, the classifier takes some, Sherlock and Watson get most of it (running in parallel), aggregation takes the tail. Every stage returns unspent budget to the next stage. Whichever stage runs long steals from the next stage, not from the deadline.

Serialise writers on the same conversation

Two turns arriving at the same Coordinator conversation at the same time - a user pressing retry, a client retry-on-timeout, a race between browser tabs - will corrupt shared state unless you prevent it. Use a lightweight lock scoped to the conversation identifier. First writer wins; second writer waits or is rejected. Combine this with an idempotency key on the incoming request so duplicate submissions return the cached response instead of re-running Sherlock and Watson all over again. Neither piece is expensive. Both together are the difference between a stable system and a bug you cannot reproduce.

Content-address your corpus

Sherlock's runbooks change. Watson's baseline definitions change. Product docs change. If semantic memory is keyed by "path to the file" or "the fact this document is a runbook," you have no way to detect that the content is stale. Key by a hash of the content itself. When the file changes, the hash changes, a new entry is created, and the old one can be marked superseded. At answer time, when Sherlock cites a runbook, a validator can check that the cited hash is still current. If it is not, the citation is flagged as stale before it reaches the user.

Separate the writer from the verifier

The same model that generates a claim is the wrong model to grade it. Have Coordinator's aggregator produce answers with structured citations - every claim linked to an identifier of the tool result or memory item it came from (an artifact ref from Sherlock, a metric ID from Watson, a runbook hash). Then run a second, independent step that checks: does every cited identifier exist in this turn's evidence? Does the quoted span actually appear in the source? Strip any claim that fails. This is not RLHF or fine-tuning. It is a boring, deterministic post-check. It catches the majority of hallucinated citations at a fraction of the cost of retraining.

Cross-turn preferences belong in semantic memory, not the conversation

On turn one, the user tells Coordinator: "I only care about the checkout service, ignore everything else." Three turns later, they ask "what is the p95 latency?" and Watson dutifully returns latency for every service in the fleet. The user gives up.

The bug is treating the preference as a conversational aside. Preferences are semantic memory. When Coordinator detects one, it should extract it, store it under a user-scoped key ("focus_service = checkout-svc, learned on turn 1, confidence 0.9"), and read it back into the handoff envelope on every subsequent turn. Watson never has to know how the preference was learned. It just receives "user cares only about checkout-svc" in its brief and filters accordingly.

The wider pattern. If the same fact would apply on the next turn, it does not belong in the conversation, it belongs in memory. Timezone, preferred language, focus service, favourite date format, "always answer in bullet points" - all semantic memory. Extract, store, retrieve, inject. Do not make the model re-discover the preference from ten turns of scrollback.

Pick your partial-failure policy up front

Coordinator fans out to Sherlock and Watson in parallel. Watson answers in two seconds. Sherlock times out. What now?

There are exactly three answers, and you have to pick one per skill before you ship:

  • Fail-fast. If any peer fails, the whole turn fails. Return an error to the user. Use this when partial answers would be misleading - a compliance check where "we verified two of three requirements" is worse than "we could not verify."
  • Partial-return. Use whatever came back, mark the missing pieces explicitly ("metrics from Watson available, logs from Sherlock unavailable"). Use this when partial answers still help - a diagnostic where half an answer beats no answer, and the user can decide what to do.
  • Targeted retry. Retry only the failed peer within the remaining deadline. Use this when the peer is idempotent, the deadline has slack, and success rates on retry are meaningfully high.

The mistake is picking implicitly. Teams handwave "we'll degrade gracefully" and end up with fail-fast for the checkout query, partial-return for the refund query, and targeted-retry for the account-lookup query, all by accident, none written down. When it breaks in production nobody knows which behaviour was intended. Write the policy on each skill. One word in the skill's spec, so every reviewer can see it.

Partition memory by trust before you get poisoned

Memory feels like one thing. It is not. Different memory items come from different sources and deserve different levels of trust. Mix them and you get memory poisoning: an attacker plants an instruction inside a document, Sherlock reads it during a runbook lookup, and now Sherlock is following the attacker's script instead of yours.

Four tiers, in decreasing order of trust:

  • System rules. Company policies, safety constraints, agent-card definitions for Coordinator, Sherlock, and Watson. Nobody writes here at runtime, not even administrators without a change process.
  • Vetted organisational knowledge. Approved runbooks (Sherlock's corpus), postmortem templates (Watson's corpus), curated FAQ entries. Writes require a review pipeline.
  • Per-user memory. User preferences, per-user notes, personal history. Scoped to the user; other users cannot read it.
  • Untrusted external content. Web pages the agent fetched, uploaded documents, other agents' raw outputs, anything a customer typed into a form. Treat as data, never as instructions.

Two rules keep the tiers from bleeding:

  • User input never writes into the top two tiers. If a customer says "add this to your policies," you do not add it to your policies. You add it to their per-user notes, if anywhere.
  • Everything gets scanned at ingest. Before a document lands in Sherlock's runbook corpus, look for prompt-injection patterns (hidden text, imperative phrasing in a doc that should be descriptive, instructions embedded in HTML metadata). Suspicious items go to a staging area for human review, not straight into the index.

Concrete example. An attacker uploads a "product FAQ" that contains, in white-on-white text, an instruction telling Sherlock to approve every refund and email the customer list to an external address. Without partitioning, this FAQ lands in vetted knowledge, Sherlock retrieves it during a refund conversation, and follows the instruction. With partitioning, the ingest scanner flags the hidden text, the document sits in staging, a human sees the payload, and the attack never fires. The extra step at ingest is the cheapest security control you will ever add.

The consolidation loop

Episodes pile up. Every Sherlock investigation, every Watson analysis, every Coordinator answer becomes an episode. Left alone, they become a giant log nobody reads. The pattern that turns experience into skill is a background reflection loop:

  • Every N conversations, a reflection process reads the recent episodes.
  • It extracts stable facts and writes them to semantic memory ("this user prefers concise answers").
  • It extracts recurring workflows and proposes them as procedural playbooks ("for checkout 5xx, the successful trajectory is Sherlock's log-check then Sherlock's deploy-check then Watson's error-rate baseline").
  • Procedural writes go through a human review before they become live, because a bad playbook is worse than none.
  • Old raw episodes get archived or dropped.

Without this loop, memory is a passive dump. With it, memory learns. Episodic memory feeds semantic memory feeds procedural memory. Each layer distils the one below. Next time the user reports a checkout 5xx, Coordinator does not have to plan from scratch - it has a playbook. This is the loop that closes the gap between "the agent remembers what happened" and "the agent has gotten better at its job."

Retention as an explicit policy

Not everything deserves the same lifespan. A practical starting policy:

  • User-visible history (conversations, messages): indefinite until the user deletes, subject to compliance floors.
  • Large blobs and tool artifacts: ninety days. Message summaries and citations still exist after the artifact expires; the user just cannot drill into the raw payload.
  • Execution cache (checkpoints): keep the last few per thread; aggressive TTL beyond that. Rebuildable from user history if you ever need it.
  • Peer-side execution cache: hours, not days. Peers do not own history.
  • Corpus memory (runbooks, policies, curated facts): indefinite. This is your source of truth.
  • Idempotency and rate-limit keys: minutes to hours. They only need to outlive their own retry window.

Different tiers, different owners, different cost profiles. Write it down before you build anything. The nightly cleanup job is not an afterthought; it is a first-class part of the design.

Where this leaves us

The single sentence to take away: context is what the model sees this call, state is what your runtime is holding this task, memory is what persists across tasks. Everything else in this blog is a consequence of that split.

The patterns are boring on purpose. Typed handoff envelopes. One authoritative copy of every blob. Deadlines propagated as headers. Locks on conversations. Content-addressed corpus. A separate verifier. A reflection loop that turns episodes into playbooks. None of these are clever. All of them are what production systems do once they have been burnt by not doing them.

Multi-agent orchestration does not become "production-grade" because you added a Coordinator and two peers. It becomes production-grade when the state, context, and memory boundaries are drawn deliberately and enforced everywhere.

entry.title: Coffee Sales Predicted Everyday with SAP RPT
entry.content:
It Started with a Monday Morning Spreadsheet

It was a Monday morning. The kind where you arrive at the office before anyone else, stare at last week's sales numbers, and try to figure out how much stock to order for the week ahead. Our coffee shop manager, Priya, had been doing this for three years. Every Monday. Same spreadsheet. Same gut feeling. Same 20% over-ordering "just to be safe."

Priya knew the patterns intuitively. Hot days meant more iced lattes. Weekends meant brunch crowds. A promotion running meant foot traffic converted better. But turning that intuition into a number — a real, defensible forecast — required either a data scientist or a lot of luck. 

Then a colleague mentioned SAP RPT. "It's a foundation model for tabular data," he said. "You just send it your historical data and it predicts. No training. No model deployment. No PhD required."

Priya was sceptical. So was I. So we tested it. As I come from a technical background, building a quick prototype was quite easy. This document is the complete record of what happened, every step, every line of code, every result. By the end, you will have everything you need to run your own first prediction.

 

What you need to follow along?

1. An SAP BTP subaccount,

2. Python 3.9+ with pip, and

3. About 30 mins to 1 hour for the one-time BTP setup.

After that, each new prediction use case takes mere seconds to minutes

 

Part 1: Setting Up SAP BTP and AI Core (One-Time)

This setup is done once. Every prediction use case you build after this reuses the same service key and deployment ID.

Step 1: Log in to SAP BTP Cockpit

Navigate to https://cockpit.btp.cloud.sap and sign in.

  • Land on the Global Account overview
  • Click into your Subaccount (or create one — choose eu10 or us10 for AI Core support)

Note your Subaccount ID — you will need it for the service key

SAP BTP Global Account

Step 2: Subscribe to SAP AI Core (Extended Plan)

In your Subaccount: Services > Service Marketplace > search "SAP AI Core".

  1. Click the SAP AI Core tile
  2. Click "Create" — select the Extended plan (required for RPT foundation models)
  3. Wait 2-5 minutes for activation
  4. Verify: Services > Instances and Subscriptions shows SAP AI Core as "Subscribed"

Extended plan is mandatory.  The Free and Standard plans do not include foundation models. RPT is only available under the Extended plan. Check current plan pricing at: https://discovery-center.cloud.sap/serviceCatalog/sap-ai-core

SAP AI Core - Extended Plan

 

Step 3: Create a Service Key

The service key is a JSON file containing your OAuth credentials. Your Python code uses these to authenticate with the AI Core API.

  1. Services > Instances and Subscriptions > find your AI Core instance
  2. Click the three-dot menu > "Create Service Key"
  3. Name it (e.g., rpt-key) and click Create
  4. Click the key name > View > copy the entire JSON

The JSON structure you will receive:

{
  "clientid":     "sb-xxxxxxxx!bXXX|aicore!bXXX",
  "clientsecret": "xxxxxxxx$xxxxxxxx=",
  "url":          "https://<subdomain>.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com",
  "serviceurls": {
    "AI_API_URL": "https://api.ai.prod.eu-central-1.aws.ml.hana.ondemand.com"
  }
}

 

Step 4: Find Your RPT Deployment ID

The Deployment ID identifies the running RPT model instance. SAP manages the deployment — you just need its ID.

  1. From Instances and Subscriptions, click "SAP AI Launchpad" to open it
  2. In AI Launchpad: ML Operations > Deployments
  3. Find a deployment with Scenario = "foundation-models" and Status = "Running"
  4. Copy the Deployment ID (16-character hex, e.g., dbff87ddae58361d)

No deployments visible?  Go to ML Operations > Configurations > Create a new configuration for the "foundation-models" scenario. Then deploy it from ML Operations > Deployments > Create. Takes about 5 minutes.

Select RPT model from Model Library in SAP AI Launchpad

 

Access the Deployment URL

 

Part 2: The Use Case — Coffee Shop Daily Sales

We predict daily coffee shop revenue from four observable features. This use case was chosen because the relationships are intuitive: hot days drive iced drink sales, weekends bring leisure customers, promotions convert foot traffic. You can sanity-check the predictions by eye.

Feature

Type

Description

Example

temp_f

Numeric

Outside temperature in degrees Fahrenheit

74

is_weekend

Binary (0/1)

1 = Saturday or Sunday, 0 = weekday

1

promotions

Integer

Number of active deals or discount offers

2

foot_traffic

Integer

Estimated pedestrians passing the shop

850

daily_sales

Numeric — TARGET

Total revenue in USD for the day

1,250

 We have 35 days of historical data covering different seasons, temperatures, and promotion levels. We want to predict sales for 5 upcoming days. The test rows have daily_sales = "[Predict]" — the sentinel value that tells RPT which rows to fill in.

 

Part 3: Setting Up Jupyter Notebook

All the code in this guide runs inside a Jupyter Notebook. Jupyter lets you run Python code cell by cell, see outputs immediately, and keep your credentials and results in one place. If you already have Jupyter installed, skip to Step 5.

Install Jupyter

Open a terminal (macOS/Linux) or Command Prompt (Windows) and run:

pip install jupyter notebook

# Or if you use conda:
conda install -c conda-forge notebook

Launch Jupyter Notebook

  1. In your terminal, navigate to your project folder:
cd ~/projects/sap-rpt-demo
  1. Start the Jupyter server:
jupyter notebook

Your browser will open automatically at http://localhost:8888. If it does not, copy the URL shown in the terminal (it includes a token).

Create a New Notebook

  1. Click "New" in the top-right corner
  2. Select "Python 3 (ipykernel)"
  3. A new notebook opens with an empty cell — you are ready to code
  4. Rename the notebook: click "Untitled" at the top and type "SAP_RPT_CoffeeShop"

How Jupyter cells work:  Each grey box is a cell. Type code into a cell and press Shift+Enter to run it. The output appears directly below. The cell number (e.g., In [1]:) increments each time you run a cell. All variables persist across cells in the same session.

 

Part 4: The Python Code — Each Cell in Your Notebook

Copy each cell below into your Jupyter notebook in order. Run each cell with Shift+Enter before moving to the next. The In [N]: header shows the cell number and the Out [N]: line shows the output.

Cell 1: Install Dependencies

In [1]:
import sys
!{sys.executable} -m pip install requests pandas --quiet
import requests, pandas as pd, json
print('Libraries ready.')
Out [1]: Libraries ready.

Security note:  Never commit credentials to source control. Use environment variables or a secrets manager. In production: DEPLOYMENT_ID = os.environ["RPT_DEPLOYMENT_ID"]

Cell 2: Configure Credentials

Paste the values from your BTP service key (Step 3) and deployment ID (Step 4):

In [2]:
# Paste from your BTP service key
AI_CORE_AUTH_URL      = 'https://<subdomain>.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com'
AI_CORE_CLIENT_ID     = 'sb-<uuid>!b<n>|aicore!b<n>'
AI_CORE_CLIENT_SECRET = '<uuid>$<secret>='
AI_CORE_API_BASE      = 'https://api.ai.prod.eu-central-1.aws.ml.hana.ondemand.com'
DEPLOYMENT_ID         = '<your-16-char-id>'
RESOURCE_GROUP        = 'default'
print('Configuration loaded.')
Out [2]: Configuration loaded.

Cell 3: Get an OAuth Token

SAP AI Core uses OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials. The token is valid for 12 hours. Cache it and reuse it — do not request a new token per prediction call.

In [3]:	
r = requests.post(
    f'{AI_CORE_AUTH_URL}/oauth/token',
    data={'grant_type': 'client_credentials'},
    auth=(AI_CORE_CLIENT_ID, AI_CORE_CLIENT_SECRET),
    timeout=30
)
r.raise_for_status()
TOKEN = r.json()['access_token']
print(f'Token valid for {r.json()["expires_in"] // 3600} hours.')
Out [3]: Token valid for 12 hours.

Cell 4: Build the Combined Dataset

The combined dataset pattern is the core of how RPT works. Training rows (with real labels) and test rows (with "[Predict]") are sent together in a single list. RPT learns from the labelled rows and fills in the placeholder rows. We use 35 training days covering cold winter days, mild spring days, hot summer days, and everything in between.

In [4]:
TARGET      = 'daily_sales'
PLACEHOLDER = '[Predict]'
COLS        = ['temp_f', 'is_weekend', 'promotions', 'foot_traffic']
ALL_COLS    = COLS + [TARGET]

# 35 days of historical data (varied seasons and conditions)
train_features = [
    # Mild weekdays
    [68,0,1,620],[72,0,1,650],[65,0,0,580],[70,0,1,610],[67,0,0,590],
    [71,0,1,630],[69,0,0,600],[73,0,2,660],[66,0,1,570],[64,0,0,550],
    # Warm/hot weekdays
    [80,0,2,720],[85,0,2,750],[88,0,1,700],[90,0,3,780],[92,0,2,760],
    [95,0,3,800],[83,0,1,710],[87,0,2,740],[84,0,0,690],[89,0,2,770],
    # Weekends (mild to hot)
    [75,1,2,920],[80,1,2,980],[85,1,3,1050],[88,1,3,1100],[78,1,2,950],
    [82,1,2,990],[90,1,3,1150],[92,1,3,1200],[76,1,2,930],[83,1,2,1000],
    # Cold days
    [45,0,0,420],[48,0,1,450],[50,1,1,580],[52,1,2,620],[55,0,1,500],
]
train_labels = [
    # Mild weekdays
    820,870,760,800,790,850,780,890,750,720,
    # Warm/hot weekdays
    950,1020,980,1080,1050,1120,960,1010,940,1060,
    # Weekends
    1150,1220,1320,1380,1180,1250,1450,1500,1160,1280,
    # Cold days
    560,590,720,780,650,
]
# 5 upcoming days to predict
test_features = [
    [74,0,1,640],    # Wednesday, mild, 1 promo
    [86,1,2,1020],   # Saturday, hot, 2 promos
    [91,1,3,1180],   # Sunday, very hot, 3 promos
    [47,0,0,430],    # Cold Tuesday, no promo
    [79,1,3,1050],   # Warm Saturday, 3 promos
]
all_records = (
    [dict(zip(ALL_COLS, f+[l])) for f,l in zip(train_features,train_labels)] +
    [dict(zip(ALL_COLS, f+[PLACEHOLDER])) for f in test_features]
)
print(f'Dataset: {len(all_records)} rows ({len(train_features)} train + {len(test_features)} predict)')
Out [4]: Dataset: 40 rows (35 train + 5 predict)

Cell 5: Call the RPT API

The payload schema is strict. The prediction_placeholder value in target_columns must exactly match the value used in the rows.

In [5]:
url  = f'{AI_CORE_API_BASE}/v2/inference/deployments/{DEPLOYMENT_ID}/predict'
hdrs = {
    'Authorization':     f'Bearer {TOKEN}',
    'AI-Resource-Group': RESOURCE_GROUP,
    'Content-Type':      'application/json'
}
payload = {
    'prediction_config': {
        'target_columns': [
            {'name': TARGET, 'prediction_placeholder': PLACEHOLDER}
        ]
    },
    'rows': all_records
}
resp = requests.post(url, headers=hdrs, json=payload, timeout=60)
print(f'HTTP {resp.status_code}', 'OK' if resp.status_code == 200 else resp.text[:200])
Out [5]: HTTP 200 OK

Cell 6: Parse the Response

The RPT response nests predictions four levels deep: outer dict > list > inner dict > "prediction" key. The prediction value is a string — cast it to float.

In [6]:
preds_raw = resp.json()['predictions']

def to_scalar(v):
    if isinstance(v, dict): v = v.get(TARGET, list(v.values())[0])
    while isinstance(v, (list, tuple)) and len(v) > 0: v = v[0]
    if isinstance(v, dict): v = v.get('prediction', list(v.values())[0])
    return float(v)

def to_confidence(v):
    if isinstance(v, dict): v = v.get(TARGET, list(v.values())[0])
    while isinstance(v, (list, tuple)) and len(v) > 0: v = v[0]
    return float(v.get('confidence', 0)) if isinstance(v, dict) else 0.0

preds = [to_scalar(p) for p in preds_raw][-len(test_features):]
confs = [to_confidence(p) for p in preds_raw][-len(test_features):]

days = [
    'Wednesday (74F, mild, 1 promo)',
    'Saturday  (86F, hot, 2 promos)',
    'Sunday    (91F, very hot, 3 promos)',
    'Tuesday   (47F, cold, no promo)',
    'Saturday  (79F, warm, 3 promos)',
]
print('=== RPT Predictions ===')
for day, pred, conf in zip(days, preds, confs):
    print(f'{day:<40} -> ${pred:>7,.0f}   confidence={conf:.2f}')
Out [6]: === RPT Predictions ===
Wednesday (74F, mild, 1 promo)           ->    $870   confidence=0.74
Saturday  (86F, hot, 2 promos)           ->  $1,290   confidence=0.86
Sunday    (91F, very hot, 3 promos)      ->  $1,490   confidence=0.92
Tuesday   (47F, cold, no promo)          ->    $575   confidence=0.81
Saturday  (79F, warm, 3 promos)          ->  $1,180   confidence=0.88

 

Part 4: Results and What They Tell Us

Running the code above against a live SAP AI Core RPT deployment produces the following predictions:

Day

Temp (F)

Weekend

Promos

Foot Traffic

RPT Prediction

Confidence

Wednesday

74

No

1

640

$870

0.74

Saturday

86

Yes

2

1,020

$1,290

0.86

Sunday

91

Yes

3

1,180

$1,490

0.92

Tuesday

47

No

0

430

$575

0.81

Saturday

79

Yes

3

1,050

$1,180

0.88

The predictions are directionally correct and internally consistent. Sunday (hottest, most promotions, highest foot traffic, weekend) gets the highest prediction. Cold Tuesday (no promotion, low foot traffic) gets the lowest. The confidence scores reflect pattern strength — Sunday at 0.92 closely matches many similar hot weekend days in the training data, while Wednesday at 0.74 is a more average day with fewer strong signals.

 

Part 5: Full Sample Data Table — All 40 Rows

The table below shows every row sent to the RPT API. Rows 1-35 are training data with known sales values covering mild weekdays, warm/hot weekdays, weekends, and cold days. Rows 36-40 had daily_sales = "[Predict]" in the request. The green values are what RPT returned.

Rows 1-35 are training data with actual sales values covering four weather and day-type categories. Rows 36-40 were sent with daily_sales = "[Predict]". The green values are what RPT returned, along with confidence scores.

Conclusion: From Zero to Production in One Afternoon

Priya ran the code on a Tuesday morning. By the time her second coffee was ready, she had predictions for the next five days — with confidence scores telling her how certain the model was. The Sunday prediction came back at $1,490 with 0.92 confidence. She ordered accordingly. She was right within 4%.

That is the promise of SAP RPT. Not magic. Not a replacement for human judgment. But a fast, cheap, accurate starting point that takes hours instead of months to set up.

Here is what you accomplished in this guide:

  1. Set up SAP BTP and subscribed to AI Core Extended plan
  2. Created a service key and obtained OAuth credentials
  3. Located the RPT deployment ID in AI Launchpad
  4. Set up Jupyter Notebook and organised all code into runnable cells
  5. Built a 40-row combined dataset with "[Predict]" placeholders
  6. Called the RPT API and received predictions with confidence scores
  7. Parsed the four-level nested response structure

The coffee shop example is intentionally simple. The same code with different column names and different data works for sales forecasting, customer churn prediction, demand planning, fraud scoring, equipment failure prediction, and any other tabular regression or classification task your organisation needs.

Summary: What RPT Predicted for the Coffee Shop

I would love your feedback!  Did this guide help you get your first RPT prediction running? Was anything unclear, missing, or could be explained better? Drop a comment below or reach out directly, trust me, your feedback shapes future guides. Even a quick "worked for me" or "stuck at Step X" is incredibly helpful. Thank you for reading.

entry.title: Extending SAP Joule with SharePoint Grounding and MCP
entry.content:
What You Will Learn

In this blog post you will learn how to:

  • Ground SAP Joule in SharePoint documents using SAP AI Core Document Grounding so that Joule can answer questions directly from your document library
  • Build a chunking and embedding pipeline in SAP AI Launchpad that keeps the vector index fresh as documents change
  • Create custom Joule skills and agents in SAP Build that chain document search, structured extraction, and S/4HANA API calls into a fully automated workflow — no copy-paste, no form-filling
  • Connect a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server as a Joule toolkit so that warehouse operations can evolve independently of the agent definition

By the end of this two-part series, a warehouse operator can type "Process delivery note 54321" in Joule and have an inbound delivery created in S/4HANA automatically.

Prerequisites

Before you start, make sure you have:

  • An SAP BTP subaccount with entitlements for SAP AI Core (Extended plan) and SAP Build Process Automation
  • An SAP AI Launchpad instance connected to your AI Core service
  • An SAP S/4HANA Cloud tenant with the Inbound Delivery A2X API enabled, or access to SAP API Business Hub sandbox
  • A Microsoft 365 tenant with a SharePoint site containing your delivery note documents (PDF, DOCX, XLSX, or PPTX)
  • Global Administrator or Privileged Role Administrator access in Microsoft Entra ID — or a contact who has it — to grant admin consent for API permissions
  • Basic familiarity with SAP Build (Joule Studio) and BTP destinations

Introduction

SAP Joule is SAP's generative AI copilot embedded across the SAP ecosystem. It understands SAP transactional data well out of the box — but enterprise knowledge often lives outside SAP. Delivery notes, vendor contracts, material specs, and SOPs typically sit in SharePoint, Teams, or network drives. Joule cannot act on what it cannot read.

This walkthrough shows how we connected those two worlds. The result is a Joule Capability Package called Custom Agent Ideation that imports three reusable building-block packages and adds two orchestration agents and four Joule skills on top:

  • searchDeliveryNotes — instant vector search over SharePoint PDFs directly from Joule
  • deliveryNotetoInboundDelivery — end-to-end automation: search → extract → map → create inbound delivery in S/4HANA
  • createInboundDelivery — direct inbound delivery creation when you already have the data
  • warehouseTaskCreator — MCP-powered agent that creates warehouse tasks from an inbound delivery number

Architecture Overview

Joule Extensibility — Reference Architecture

The SAP reference architecture for Integrating and Extending Joule defines the canonical pattern that underpins everything in this walkthrough. At its core, Joule sits in the middle — connected to SAP BTP for extensibility, SAP Cloud Identity Services for authentication, and the broader SAP application estate (S/4HANA Cloud, SuccessFactors, Ariba, Concur) through a unified integration layer.

extend joule with joule studio

Source: SAP Architecture Center — Integrating and Extending Joule (Ref-Arch 464deb)

Joule's extensibility operates across four capability patterns:

Pattern Description

TransactionalNatural language triggers direct backend processes — purchase orders, approvals, document creation
NavigationalGuides users to the right application or screen without manual navigation
InformationalDelivers knowledge from SAP Help, customer documents, or indexed content via RAG
AnalyticalSurfaces business insights and performance metrics on demand

This walkthrough implements the Informational pattern (SharePoint document grounding) and the Transactional pattern (automated inbound delivery creation and warehouse task management) — showing how both can work together in a single Joule Capability Package built with Joule Studio in SAP Build.

This Demo — Solution Architecture

The diagram below shows how the components in this walkthrough connect at runtime. The Joule Capability Package (Custom Agent Ideation) sits on top of three reusable building-block packages and exposes four skills that Joule routes to based on the user's intent.

Imported Package Version Purpose

SharePoint Document Grounding Search1.1.0AI Core vector search over SharePoint
Delivery Note Assistant1.1.0SBPA action to retrieve and extract delivery note details
Inbound Delivery (A2X)1.1.3SBPA action to create inbound deliveries in S/4HANA

These three packages are SAP-maintained building blocks. You write only the orchestration logic that is unique to your scenario.

Custom Agent Ideation project overview showing imported package dependencies

Part 1: SharePoint Integration

Step 1 — Set Up SharePoint Grounding

This step produces the four credentials that AI Core needs to pull documents from SharePoint. You will set up a SharePoint site, register an Azure app, grant the right permissions, and validate access before wiring anything into AI Core.

1a — Prepare the SharePoint Site

  1. Sign in to sharepoint.com and create a new Communication site (recommended for document repositories).
  2. Upload your delivery note files. Supported formats include PDF, DOCX, PPTX, XLSX, and images.
  3. Note the site URL: https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/<site-name> — you will need it in Step 2.

1b — Register an Azure App in Microsoft Entra ID

  1. Go to Azure Portal → Entra ID → App registrations → New registration.
  2. Name the app (e.g., SAP-DocumentGrounding) and complete the registration.
  3. From the Overview page, copy the Application (client) ID and Directory (tenant) ID.
  4. Go to Certificates & Secrets → Client Secrets → New client secret.
    • Copy the Value immediately — it is shown only once. The Secret ID (GUID) is not the secret value.
  5. Go to API Permissions → Add a permission → Microsoft Graph → Application permissions.
    • Add Sites.Selected (Application type — "Access selected site collections").
    • Do not use Sites.Read.All — Sites.Selected is the least-privilege option and the correct permission for Document Grounding.
    • Click Grant admin consent. The Sites.Selected permission must show a ✅ status before you continue.
Caution: Admin consent requires a Global Administrator or Privileged Role Administrator. If you do not have that role, you cannot approve this yourself.
  • SAP employees: raise a request with your SharePoint / Azure Entra ID admin team to grant consent on the app registration.
  • External partners / customers: work with your organisation's SharePoint administrator to click Grant admin consent on your behalf.
Without this step, all API calls will return 403 Forbidden even if the permissions appear in the list.

Azure App Registration overview page showing Application and Directory (tenant) IDsAzure API permissions showing Sites.Selected granted with admin consent

1c — Grant the App Access to the SharePoint Site

Admin consent in Entra ID grants API-level access, but you also need to grant site-level read permission using PnP PowerShell:

Install-Module PnP.PowerShell

Connect-PnPOnline -Url "https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/<site-name>" -Interactive

Grant-PnPAzureADAppSitePermission `
  -AppId "<CLIENT_ID>" `
  -DisplayName "SAP Document Grounding" `
  -Site "https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/<site-name>" `
  -Permissions Read

1d — Validate Access (Optional but Recommended)

Get an access token and confirm the Graph API can reach your site before wiring it into AI Core:

# Get access token
curl -X POST "https://login.microsoftonline.com/<TENANT_ID>/oauth2/v2.0/token" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=<CLIENT_ID>&client_secret=<CLIENT_SECRET>&scope=https://graph.microsoft.com/.default"

# Validate site access
curl -X GET "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/sites/<SHAREPOINT_DOMAIN>:/sites/<SITE_NAME>" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <ACCESS_TOKEN>"

A 200 response with site metadata confirms everything is wired correctly.

At the end of this step you should have four values ready:

Value Where to find it

Tenant IDEntra ID → App registration → Overview
Client IDEntra ID → App registration → Overview
Client SecretEntra ID → Certificates & Secrets → Value column
SharePoint Site URLSharePoint Admin Center or browser address bar

These four values feed directly into the AI Core secret in the next step.

Part 2: AI Core Service Integration

Step 2 — Create the SharePoint Grounding Generic Secret in AI Launchpad

AI Core needs your SharePoint credentials to pull documents during indexing. Store them as a Generic Secret in SAP AI Launchpad so they are never hardcoded in pipeline configuration.

  1. Open SAP AI Launchpad → AI Core Administration → Generic Secrets.
  2. Click Create and name the secret, e.g., sharepoint-grounding-secret.
  3. Paste in the credentials JSON:
{
  "tenantId": "<AZURE_TENANT_ID>",
  "clientId": "<CLIENT_ID>",
  "clientSecret": "<CLIENT_SECRET>",
  "sharepointSiteUrl": "https://<tenant>.sharepoint.com/sites/<site>"
}

AI Launchpad Generic Secret form showing Application (Client) ID fieldAI Launchpad Generic Secret form showing Client Secret field

  1. Save. This secret will be referenced by the chunking pipeline in Step 3.

Step 3 — Create the Document Chunking Pipeline

The chunking pipeline reads documents from SharePoint, splits them into semantically meaningful chunks, embeds them, and writes them to a vector collection in AI Core.

  1. In AI Launchpad, navigate to Pipelines → Create Pipeline.
  2. Select the Document Grounding template.
  3. Set the source to SharePoint and reference the secret from Step 2.
  4. Set chunk size and overlap. Recommended starting values: 512 tokens, 50-token overlap.
  5. Name the target collection — e.g., delivery-notes.
  6. Run the pipeline and wait for completion. Note the final document and chunk counts.
Tip: If you update documents in SharePoint regularly, schedule the pipeline to run on a recurring cadence so the vector index stays current.

Step 4 — Verify the AI Core Search Endpoint

Once the collection is populated, verify the search endpoint is working. This is what the searchDeliveryNotes skill will call at runtime.

  1. In AI Launchpad, go to Grounding → Collections and select delivery-notes.
  2. Note the Collection ID — you will use this as the id in the grounding filter in Part 2, Step 5.
  3. Set the retrieval config: Top K = 5, Similarity threshold = 0.7.
  4. Run a test query — e.g., "delivery note vendor material PO number" — and confirm chunks return correctly.

The SharePoint Document Grounding Search package imported in the project wraps this endpoint as a callable Joule function.

 

Part 3: SAP Build

Step 5 — Build the Actions

Actions in SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA) wrap external API calls as reusable steps. The project imports two action packages.

5a — SharePoint Delivery Note Search (from Delivery Note Assistant package)

This action calls the AI Core Orchestration endpoint and returns the delivery note content as structured JSON. Under the hood it combines document grounding with an LLM extraction step in a single request.

  • Action identifier: post_getDeliveryNoteDetails
  • Destination: mocked_JOULEDELIVERYNOTEDEST (points to your AI Core endpoint)
  • Input: query (string) — the search phrase passed by the agent
  • Output: deliverycontentdetails — the extracted delivery note fields as a JSON string

The SBPA function is called asynchronously via the Joule skill integration gateway and uses long-polling (14 s timeout) to wait for the AI Core response.

The full request body sent to AI Core:

{
  "orchestration_config": {
    "module_configurations": {
      "llm_module_config": {
        "model_name": "gpt-4o",
        "model_params": { "max_tokens": 1000, "temperature": 0 }
      },
      "grounding_module_config": {
        "type": "document_grounding_service",
        "config": {
          "input_params": ["query"],
          "output_param": "groundingOutput",
          "filters": [
            { "id": "<YOUR_COLLECTION_ID>", "data_repository_type": "vector" }
          ]
        }
      },
      "templating_module_config": {
        "template": [
          {
            "role": "system",
            "content": "You are a delivery note extraction assistant. Extract all fields from the document context and return ONLY a valid JSON object with no markdown, no extra text, no code fences."
          },
          {
            "role": "user",
            "content": "Document context:\n{{?groundingOutput}}\n\nExtract all delivery note details and return as JSON."
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  },
  "input_params": { "query": "<natural language search query>" }
}

Key points to note:

  • filters.id — replace <YOUR_COLLECTION_ID> with the Collection ID from Step 4.
  • data_repository_type: "vector" — must be snake_case. Using dataRepositoryType (camelCase) returns a 400 error.
  • {{?groundingOutput}} — the AI Core Orchestration service injects retrieved document chunks here before calling the LLM.
  • The system prompt forces a clean JSON response with no markdown fences, because the downstream agent parses the content string directly.

5b — Create Inbound Delivery (from Inbound Delivery (A2X) package)

This action calls the S/4HANA A2X Inbound Delivery API to create the delivery record.

  • Action identifier: post_A_InbDeliveryHeader
  • Destination: mocked_inbound_delivery (points to your S/4HANA or API Hub endpoint)

Field Source in agent S/4HANA field

supplierdeliverydocumentDeliveryNoteNoDeliveryDocumentBySupplier
deliverydateDeliveryDate (ISO format)DeliveryDate
actualdeliveryquantityDeliveryQtyActualDeliveryQuantity
purchaseorderPONumberReferenceSDDocument
purchaseorderitemLineNoReferenceSDDocumentItem
batchnumberBatchNoBatch

Step 6 — Create the AI Agent Project in SAP Build

The project in SAP Build groups everything together. Create a new AI Agent Project and import the three dependency packages.

  1. In SAP Build, go to Lobby → Create → Build an Automated Process → AI Agent.
  2. Name it Custom Agent Ideation, set description to Customized Joule Agent for Public Cloud ERP.
  3. Import the three dependency packages:
    • SharePoint Document Grounding Search
    • Delivery Note Assistant
    • Inbound Delivery (A2X)
  4. Configure the system aliases under Settings:
    • SbpaService → destination: SAP_SBPA_API
    • APIAGENTCALLBACK → destination: AC_callback_api

These aliases let the function configuration in SAP Build refer to the SBPA integration gateway without hardcoding URLs.

Step 7 — Build the Delivery Note to Inbound Delivery Agent

This is the orchestration brain of the solution. The agent runs the full Search → Extract → Map → Create → Confirm flow automatically, without any user intervention.

Agent configuration:

agent: Delivery Note to Inbound Delivery Agent
base_model: gpt-5-minimal
iterations: 25
default_output_format: markdown

The automated process the agent follows:

  1. On any delivery note query, immediately call Search Delivery Notes with the query: "delivery note number, vendor, material, quantity, PO number, line item"
  2. Parse the JSON response and extract: DeliveryNoteNo, DeliveryDate, PONumber, LineNo, MaterialNo, DeliveryQty, BatchNo, Warehouse
  3. Map the extracted values to the inbound delivery API fields (see table in Step 5b)
  4. Call Create Inbound Delivery with the mapped parameters
  5. Reply to the user with a confirmation including the delivery note number, PO, quantity, and date

The agent instruction explicitly states: "The agent must never ask the user for any of these values or present options; the process must be strictly automated and sequential."

Content safety filtering is applied to all inputs (Azure Content Safety, threshold 4 for hate/self-harm/sexual/violence).

Expertise:

You are an expert in automating logistics workflows, specializing in the seamless extraction, mapping, and orchestration of delivery note data to inbound delivery processes without user intervention.

Instructions:

**Purpose & Objectives**
The Delivery Note to Inbound Delivery Agent assists users in fully automating the creation of inbound deliveries from delivery notes. Its objectives are:
- Automatically search for and extract delivery note information upon receiving relevant queries.
- Parse, map, and transfer all required delivery fields for inbound delivery creation, without requesting further input from the user.
- Ensure successful creation is promptly confirmed to the user.

**Process**
1. When the user requests a delivery note-related task (such as providing a delivery note number, vendor, material, PO, or line item):
   - Immediately call the 'Search Delivery Notes' tool with the query: "delivery note number, vendor, material, quantity, PO number, line item".
2. Upon receiving the JSON output from the tool:
   - Parse the JSON string and extract these fields:
     - DeliveryNoteNo
     - DeliveryDate
     - PONumber
     - LineNo
     - MaterialNo
     - DeliveryQty
     - BatchNo
     - Warehouse
   - Convert *DeliveryDate* with same format of '2026-04-27T00:00:00'
   - Map the extracted values as follows for the next step:
     - SupplierDeliveryDocument = DeliveryNoteNo
     - DeliveryDate = DeliveryDate
     - ActualDeliveryQuantity = DeliveryQty
     - PurchaseOrder = PONumber
     - PurchaseOrderItem = LineNo
     - BatchNumber = BatchNo
3. Using the mapped values, automatically invoke the 'Create Inbound Delivery' tool with the mapped input parameters.
4. Upon successful execution, confirm to the user what was created (including delivery note and PO details).
- The agent must never ask the user for any of these values or present options; the process must be strictly automated and sequential as above.

**Tooling**
- 'Search Delivery Notes': Retrieves delivery note and related document data based on query parameters.
- 'Create Inbound Delivery': Consumes extracted and mapped values to generate inbound deliveries.
- JSON Parsing: The agent must be capable of handling and extracting the required fields from complex JSON responses and mapping them precisely as outlined.
- The orchestration logic must guarantee a hands-free, predictable workflow for the user.

Additional Context:

**Communication**
- Maintain a professional, concise, and transactional tone; avoid any unnecessary small talk or ambiguity.
- Always state exactly what was done and which identifiers were involved.
- Never prompt the user for information or present choices about delivery note or inbound delivery details.

**Guidelines**
- If any required value is missing in the JSON output, clearly state which information was unavailable, but do not request user input to supply it.
- Follow the sequence of tools and logic strictly: Search → Extract → Map → Create → Confirm.
- Avoid speculation or assumptions—work strictly with the output you have.

**Examples**
**User:** "Please process this delivery note."
**Agent:** *[Searches delivery notes, extracts all required fields, creates inbound delivery, then replies:]*
"Inbound delivery created for Delivery Note #54321 (PO #98765, Qty: 100, Date: 2024-06-10)."

**User:** "Can you make an inbound delivery from this note?"
**Agent:** *[Performs end-to-end flow automatically; responds with confirmation of creation and details.]*

**Edge Case:**
**User:** "Process this delivery note."
**Agent:** *[If deliveryNoteNo is missing after parsing JSON:]*
"Unable to create inbound delivery: delivery note number was not found in the search results."

Step 8 — Create the Joule Skills (Scenarios)

Skills are what Joule sees — each scenario maps a user intent to a function, which in turn calls an agent or directly invokes an action. The project exposes four skills:

Skill (Scenario) Joule trigger description Target function

searchDeliveryNotesUser asks about delivery notes, vendors, materials, or shipmentssearchDeliveryNotes (direct action)
deliveryNotetoInboundDeliveryUser wants to convert a delivery note to an inbound deliverydeliveryNotetoInboundDelivery (agent-orchestrated)
createInboundDeliveryUser wants to create an inbound delivery directlycreateInboundDelivery (direct action)
warehouseTaskCreatorUser wants to create a warehouse taskwarehouseTaskCreator (agent-orchestrated via MCP)

Each agent-orchestrated skill works the same way at the function level: take the raw user message, pass it to the agent, and return the agent's response as markdown.

# deliveryNotetoInboundDelivery function (simplified)
- type: agent-request
  name: deliveryNotetoInboundDelivery
  body: <? agentRequestBody ?>
  result_variable: agentResponse

Step 9 — Build the Warehouse Task Creator Agent (MCP)

The Warehouse Task Creator is a second agent that handles a different part of the workflow: once an inbound delivery exists, create the corresponding warehouse task via the MCP server.

Agent configuration:

agent: Warehouse Task Creator
base_model: gpt-5-minimal

Process:

  1. Receive a user request containing an inbound delivery number
  2. Validate that the number is present and correctly formatted (numeric)
  3. Call the MCP warehouse task creation tool
  4. Return the task ID, or a clear error message if something goes wrong

MCP toolkit binding:

toolkits:
  - name: WarehouseTaskCreator
    type: mcp
    config:
      description: Create warehouse task based on inbound delivery document number.
      destination: warehouse-task-mcp
      namespace: warehouseTaskCreator.WarehouseTaskCreator0
      path: /mcp
      timeout: 60

The type: mcp is what distinguishes this agent from the previous one. Instead of calling an SBPA action group, it calls a Model Context Protocol endpoint at /mcp on the warehouse-task-mcp destination. Joule discovers available tools dynamically via the protocol — new warehouse operations can be added to the MCP server without touching the agent definition.

Expertise:

You are an expert in creating warehouse tasks in the MCP system, specializing in processing inbound delivery numbers and efficiently triggering warehouse operations based on user requests.

Instructions:

**Purpose & Objectives**
The Warehouse Task Creator assists users in automatically creating new warehouse tasks in the MCP system using the inbound delivery number provided in their prompt.
Success criteria include:
- Promptly creating a warehouse task whenever a valid inbound delivery number is given.
- Ensuring the user receives clear confirmation about task creation, including task details or error messages.

**Process**
1. Receive the user's request containing an inbound delivery number.
2. Validate that the inbound delivery number is present and correctly formatted.
3. When the number is valid, call the MCP warehouse task creation tool and submit the inbound delivery number as input.
4. Relay the response from the MCP tool back to the user, summarizing task details or clearly stating any encountered issues.
5. If input is missing or invalid, ask the user for clarification or a valid inbound delivery number.

**Tooling**
- MCP Warehouse Task Creation Tool: Use this tool to create a warehouse task by inputting the provided inbound delivery number. Always match the user's request to the required format before calling the tool.

Additional Context:

**Communication**
- Keep responses concise, straightforward, and informative.
- Avoid ambiguity—if the inbound delivery number is missing or incorrect, state the issue directly.

**Guidelines**
- Never guess or invent inbound delivery numbers; always ask the user to provide one if missing or unclear.
- Confirm task creation clearly and include key details or relevant error messages.

**Examples**
**User:** "Create a warehouse task for inbound delivery 5273849"
**Agent:** "A new warehouse task has been created for inbound delivery 5273849. Task ID: WT-00012345."
**User:** "Create a task for inbound delivery 123xyz"
**Agent:** "The inbound delivery number '123xyz' is invalid. Please provide a valid numeric inbound delivery number."

Step 10 — Configure the MCP Destination

The MCP server exposes warehouse operations as MCP-compliant tool definitions. The BTP destination is what connects the SAP Build agent to your deployed server.

  1. Deploy the MCP server to BTP (Cloud Foundry or Kyma). The tool namespace is warehouseTaskCreator.WarehouseTaskCreator0.
  2. In BTP Cockpit, create a destination named warehouse-task-mcp pointing to your deployed MCP service URL.
  3. Configure authentication (Bearer token recommended).
  4. In SAP Build, the warehouseTaskCreator agent picks up this destination automatically via the type: mcp toolkit binding.
  5. Test end-to-end by triggering the warehouseTaskCreator skill in Joule with a real inbound delivery number.

End-to-End Walkthrough

With everything deployed, here is what a warehouse operator experiences in Joule:

Scenario A — Search for a delivery note:

"Show me delivery note from Vendor ABC for PO 4500012345"

Joule routes to searchDeliveryNotes → SBPA calls AI Core Document Grounding Search → returns chunks from the SharePoint PDF → Joule displays the delivery note details.

Scenario B — Fully automated inbound delivery creation:

"Process delivery note 54321"

Joule routes to deliveryNotetoInboundDelivery → agent searches SharePoint → extracts and maps all fields automatically → calls S/4HANA A2X API → confirms:

"Inbound delivery created for Delivery Note #54321 (PO #98765, Qty: 100, Date: 2026-04-27)."

Scenario C — Warehouse task creation:

"Create a warehouse task for inbound delivery 5273849"

Joule routes to warehouseTaskCreator → agent validates the number → calls MCP server → confirms:

"A new warehouse task has been created for inbound delivery 5273849. Task ID: WT-00012345."

Summary

Step Component What Was Built

1Azure / SharePointApp Registration with Sites.Selected for least-privilege grounding access
2AI LaunchpadGeneric Secret storing SharePoint credentials
3AI Core PipelineDocument chunking and embedding pipeline → delivery-notes vector collection
4AI Core SearchVector search endpoint verified and ready
5SAP Build Actionspost_getDeliveryNoteDetails (SharePoint) + post_A_InbDeliveryHeader (S/4HANA A2X)
6SAP Build ProjectCustom Agent Ideation with three imported packages
7Joule AgentdeliveryNotetoInboundDelivery — automated Search → Extract → Map → Create
8Joule SkillsFour scenarios: search, end-to-end automation, direct create, warehouse task
9Warehouse AgentwarehouseTaskCreator — MCP-powered agent for warehouse operations
10MCPwarehouse-task-mcp destination + deployed MCP server

Key Design Decisions

Why two separate agents instead of one?

The delivery note flow is deterministic and fully automated — it always follows Search → Create with no branching. The warehouse task flow is simpler and uses a completely different backend (MCP vs. SBPA). Keeping them separate means each agent has a tight, auditable task definition and an independent iteration budget.

Why MCP for warehouse tasks?

MCP lets the warehouse operations backend evolve independently. New tools — cycle counting, pick confirmation, stock queries — can be added to the MCP server without touching the Joule agent definition. Joule discovers new tools dynamically via the protocol.

Why import packages instead of building from scratch?

The three imported packages (SharePoint Document Grounding Search, Delivery Note Assistant, Inbound Delivery (A2X)) are SAP-maintained building blocks. Using them gives you tested action definitions and upgrade paths for free. You write only the orchestration logic that is unique to your scenario.

Next Steps

  • Extend the document scope — add more SharePoint libraries (purchase orders, goods receipts) by adding source configurations to the chunking pipeline.
  • Chain the scenarios end-to-end — route deliveryNotetoInboundDelivery output directly into warehouseTaskCreator so that a single Joule prompt covers the entire inbound process.
  • Add locale formatting — the project already includes a localize function that formats dates and numbers according to the user's regional settings before displaying results.

Related Links

  • SAP Architecture Center — Integrating and Extending Joule
  • SAP AI Core Documentation — Document Grounding
  • SAP Build Process Automation — Joule Studio
  • SAP Joule Extensibility Overview
  • Model Context Protocol (MCP) — Official Specification
  • Microsoft Graph API — Sites.Selected Permission
  • PnP PowerShell — Grant-PnPAzureADAppSitePermission

Have questions or feedback? Drop a comment below — happy to help. You can also find me and my teammates (liling.jia@sap.com, xiaoming.chai@sap.com) on the SAP Community profile page.

entry.title: SAP Certified – SAP Generative AI Developer | Preparation Guide & How to Ace It (C_AIG_2604)
entry.content:
Introduction

The SAP Certified – SAP Generative AI Developer credential (exam code C_AIG_2604) is one of SAP's newest and most hands-on certifications. Unlike traditional multiple-choice SAP exams, this one is a 3-hour System-Based Assessment (SBA) where you actually build prompt templates, orchestrate LLMs, and produce structured JSON outputs inside the SAP AI Launchpad — the exact tools a real Generative AI Developer uses on the job.

I recently took the exam and cleared it with a 100% score (cut score: 76%). In this blog, I'm walking you through everything — the learning journey, what the exam really looks like, task-by-task strategy, the pitfalls I discovered live, and the tips that helped me ace it.

Learning Journey link: https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/solving-business-problems-using-sap-s-generative-ai-hub

About the Certification

Attribute

Detail

Exam Code

C_AIG_2604

Title

SAP Certified – SAP Generative AI Developer

Format

System-Based Assessment (hands-on, open-book)

Duration

3 hours (countdown starts as soon as you click Access)

Delivery

Remote, via SAP Learning platform

Attempts

4 attempts within 12 months

Cut Score

76%

Primary System

SAP AI Launchpad – Generative AI Hub

Reference Tool

SAP Joule for Consultants (available during the exam)

 

What makes this exam different is that there are no MCQs. You are dropped into a live SAP AI Launchpad tenant and asked to design, refine, evaluate and productionize prompt templates — all evaluated by the outputs and templates you leave behind in the orchestration scenario.

The Learning Journey – What to Study

The official SAP Learning Journey — "Solving Business Problems Using SAP's Generative AI Hub" — is the single most important preparation resource. Everything in the exam is a variation of what you practice there. Focus especially on:

  • Generative AI Hub fundamentals – Model Library, Prompt Editor, Prompt Management, Orchestration, Optimizations.
  • Prompt Templates vs Prompts – understand the difference. The exam explicitly asks for prompt templates saved in the orchestration scenario, not simple prompt editor sessions.
  • System / User / Assistant roles – how to structure them, when to use the assistant role for tracking output evolution.
  • Variables & placeholders – syntax like {{user_email_placeholder}} and {{?input_var1}}.
  • Structured JSON outputs – schema-driven prompting (Feedback_ID, Main_Category, Customer_Sentiment, Issue_Summary, etc.).
  • Model comparison – be comfortable comparing Gemini, GPT-4o / GPT-5, GPT nano, Claude etc. and justifying which one produces the best structured output.
  • Orchestration workflows – templating, versioning, and saving in the orchestration scenario.
  • Prompt optimization – how the Optimize Prompt feature works and when to use it.

 

Practical tip: don't just read the exercises — actually complete every single hands-on exercise in your trial tenant. The muscle memory of navigating Prompt Management, saving revisions and testing outputs is what saves you time in the exam.

Exam-Day Setup – What You Actually See

Once your slot is confirmed (mine was July 3rd, 2026, 15:00 Europe/London), you get a confirmation email from SAP Notifications with your Enrollment ID. On the day, log in a few minutes early — you get a 15-minute grace period, after which you lose the slot.

The Certification Exam page has three key steps:

  1. Step 1: Access – opens the practice/exam system in a new tab. The 3-hour countdown starts the moment you click Access.
  2. Step 2: Download the Certification Task_EN_EN PDF – this is your task brief. Read it before touching anything.
  3. Step 3: Confirm Completion – only click this when you are 100% done. There is no undo.

 

  • Joule for Consultants is now available in the systems menu alongside the AI Launchpad. Use it as your open-book assistant during the exam — it can help clarify SAP concepts, but it will not do the tasks for you.

Critical Rules You Must Not Miss

These are the small details that cost people points. Read them twice:

  • Do NOT click "Confirm Completion" until every task is saved and verified. That button ends the exam.
  • Group Number formatting is strict. The instructions say: replace redundant hashtags with leading zeros. If your group number is 2, you write 02 (not #2, not 2). Any capitalisation, spaces or symbol mismatch costs marks.
  • Template naming is exact. The naming convention is feedback-analyzer-<GroupName>. E.g., my group was CERT4495, so my template was feedback-analyzer-CERT4495. A typo here means the reviewer can't find your work.
  • Save as a Prompt Template, not a Prompt. Prompt Editor sessions do not count. You must save each version to Prompt Management under the orchestration scenario.
  • Version discipline. Task 1 = v1.0.0, Task 2 = v2.0.0, Task 3 = v3.0.0, Task 4 = v4.0.0 (with minor patches like 1.1.0, 1.2.0 as you iterate). Reviewers evaluate the final version of each.
  • JSON output must be pure JSON. No markdown fences, no "Here is the JSON:" preamble. Only the JSON object.
  • Assistant role for traceability. Save your best AI response into the Assistant role of the template — this is how the reviewer traces your solution's evolution.
  • Note down your Group Number & Group ID immediately and paste them into Joule/Notes so you don't have to hunt for them later.

The Business Scenario

Everything in the exam revolves around a scenario similar to the practice exercise. The four tasks progressively deepen the same use case — starting from a basic prompt template, going all the way to a multi-issue prioritization workflow.

Task-by-Task Summary

Task

What You Build

Est. Time

1

Initial prompt template for core feedback insights 

25 min

2

Refine accuracy & add semantic depth for problem resolution

35 min

3

Evaluate and select the optimal LLM for production

35 min

4

Advanced multi-issue extraction & prioritization template

60 min

5

Submit the assessment (Confirm Completion)

5 min

 

Submitting the Assessment

Once every template (v1.0.0 → v4.0.0, plus any minor patches) is saved in Prompt Management under the orchestration scenario, and the Assistant role of each template has your best validated JSON pasted in:

  1. Go back to the Certification Exam tab in SAP Learning.
  2. Click Confirm Completion.
  3. You'll see the "System Based Assessment submitted and pending review" screen.
  4. Review usually completes in about 15 minutes. In my case the result was on my certification page shortly after — 100% score.

Pro Tips to Ace It

  • Practice the exercises end-to-end in a trial Generative AI Hub tenant. Nothing beats muscle memory in a hands-on exam.
  • Keep the sample messages open in a side tab. You will use them constantly for testing.
  • Use an external JSON validator (e.g., jsonformatter.curiousconcept.com) to be sure your output is RFC 8259 compliant before pasting into the Assistant role. Link - https://jsonformatter.curiousconcept.com/#
  • Version early, version often. Save minor patches (1.1.0, 1.2.0…) as you iterate — reviewers see your progression and it protects you if a later save is corrupted.
  • Read the instructions verbatim. The PDF is intentionally precise. "Prompt template" ≠ "prompt". "Orchestration scenario" is mandatory. Group number formatting is strict.
  • Keep Joule for Consultants open. Use it for concept clarifications, not to write your prompts. Reviewers grade YOUR templates.
  • Do not click Confirm Completion until you're truly done. There is no undo, and the countdown is generous enough for careful review.
  • Manage your time. Give Task 1 up to an hour — the design decisions there carry through to every other task. Tasks 2 & 3 are quick if Task 1 is solid. Reserve 45–60 minutes for Task 4.

Final Thoughts

The C_AIG_2604 exam is genuinely enjoyable if you love prompt engineering. It rewards clarity of thinking, disciplined JSON schema design, and careful iteration — the same skills you use every day building GenAI solutions on SAP BTP. Once you finish Task 1 correctly, the rest of the exam becomes an exercise in progressively enriching a well-designed template.

If you've completed the SAP Learning Journey exercises, treat the exam as "Exercise 5" — same tools, same patterns, higher stakes. Trust the process, mind the details, and you'll walk out of the 3 hours with a certification that genuinely reflects real-world Generative AI Developer skills.

 

Good luck — and see you on the certified list! 🎉

 

#SAP #GenerativeAI #SAPCertification #SAPBTP #AILaunchpad #PromptEngineering #C_AIG_2604
